text: Hi, I'm Nicole from AWS.
Hi, I'm Brandon O'Daniel from Xylem, and this is my architecture.
Welcome, Brandon.
So, I am a huge gardener that enjoys using IoT devices to collect metrics about my actual garden's performance.
I'm really curious about this serverless event-driven IoT collection solution that you've built.
So, how did we get here?
Yes, this solution was designed for Xylem, which is a worldwide water company,
specifically for the census brand of Xylem, which does a lot of smart metering and electric, water, and gas distribution networks.
So, the solution we designed is called the Xylem Data Lake, formerly known as the Census Utility Data Lake.
And specifically, in this solution, we have data stores that send files from various IT systems up into our Amazon S3 data storage,
where there are S3 events that trigger lambdas, which loads data into Amazon Redshift clusters for us.

Additionally, we have situations where some of our data-producing applications goes through and publishes REST APIs, which are called via AWS LandVis, to pull data and then insert into Amazon Redshift.
And when I say Amazon Redshift here, it's actually about 11 different Amazon Redshift clusters spread off across three different geographies worldwide.
Okay. So, you're using a combination of push and pull to collect that data into Redshift.
Mm-hmm.
Well, can you tell us about your considerations? Why Redshift in this solution?
The reason why we picked Redshift was because we needed a columnar data store that was very performant.
And also, too, we needed the ability through to data governance requirements from our customers to physically separate our customers' data from each other customer.
Mm-hmm.
So, that led us away from solutions such as using Athena with Parquet storage on S3, which we use in other places in our internal data lakes.
But in terms of customer facing, we needed Redshift.
Also, too, we wanted to take advantage of the reserved instance pricing of Redshift, which saved us a significant amount of money which we could pass along to our customers.
Now, I'm curious then, I do see that we're using our EventBridge service here. So, how are you actually leveraging this and your solution for moving data around?
Yeah, it was wonderful. So, about the time we were designing a batch process or a batch scheduler, Amazon EventBridge scheduler was released.
And we said, this is great. Why don't we go through and try to use this? Because we love serverless on our team.
Because serverless decreases the total cost of labor and amount of maintenance that we need to do as a development team.

We use Amazon EventBridge scheduler to write batch jobs to pull data from Amazon Redshift instances. And then those batch jobs can either send reports, they can send data files out through Amazon Simple Email Service or SES.
We can go through and publish data stores over to our AWS Transfer SFTP instance within our company. And there's also places where customers can pull data. So, for instance, we have a Angular.
Angular web application. That's hosted on CloudFront where it makes calls over to Lambda in order to pull data up into Redshift. So, customers can go through and generate their own reports.
So, I'm hearing a shift towards making sure that you have managed or serverless services. So, you don't have to do the unfiltered lifting of managing that piece.
Mm-hm. And I'm also hearing that you have another way to actually allow your consumers to consume this data.
Mm-hm. How do you apply security to this solution?
So, our customers, what they do is they come in through Amazon CloudFront through our Angular web application. And from there, they are authenticated via a Keycloak instance.
All of our Angular web is put through a Web application firewall. And we use API gateway, secure through Web application firewall, in order to be a wrapper around the Lambdas.
We also use such things as like SSH key sharing and CyberArk and that sort of thing in order to provide customers secure access over into like the SFTP side of things.
Okay. Very good. Well, thank you so much for sharing your architecture with us.
